title: An efficient Progressive Swapping to the Middle distribution protocol adapted to imperfect quantum memories in quantum networks
authors: Claire Mesny, Fabrice Guillemin, Claire Goursaud
abstract: The distribution of entangled pairs of photons on the links composing a quantum network, combined with Bell state measurements and teleportation, is the basic apparatus to transfer quantum bits (qubits) over long distances. Entanglement distribution establishes an end-to-end entangled pair while consuming intermediate pairs on links and holding them for a certain time period. The technical literature identifies two main kinds of protocols, parallel and sequential ones, the latter having an advantage in resource consumption over the former. In this paper, we introduce an efficient swapping protocol called Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) as it combines the existing Progressive Swapping (PS) protocol from both extremities of a path that meet in the middle where the received pairs are swapped. We compare PSM with two parallel protocols and PS; in our evaluation, we take into account imperfect memories and fidelity degradation. We demonstrate that PSM yields a much better link probability than PS while keeping a reasonable link fidelity, and shows an advantage in resource consumption over other protocols.
summary: This paper introduces the Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) protocol for quantum networks to improve long-distance qubit transmission. By comparing PSM against other established protocols, the authors demonstrate that PSM offers superior resource efficiency and link probability while maintaining high fidelity, especially when quantum memories are imperfect. This advances the practical feasibility of quantum networking.
main_problem: The paper addresses the challenge of efficiently transferring quantum bits (qubits) over long distances in quantum networks by optimizing entanglement distribution and swapping protocols.
main_result: The proposed Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) protocol is shown to be highly resource-efficient, yielding better link probabilities and lower resource consumption compared to existing protocols like PS and BS, especially under imperfect memory conditions.
method: The authors compare PSM against several established protocols (PS, BS, HD) by calculating key performance metrics like link probability, link fidelity, and minimum required resource consumption.
model_or_system: The system modeled is a quantum network utilizing entangled photon pairs distributed over links, requiring Bell state measurements and teleportation for end-to-end qubit transfer. The analysis incorporates decoherence from imperfect quantum memories and fiber loss.
key_observables: Link probability (success rate), Link Fidelity ($F$), and Resource Consumption (minimum number of generated pairs required).
important_parameters: Memory decay time ($	au$), time-slot ($\Delta$), and the swap success probability ($p_{swap} \approx 0.9$).
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes exponential decay for memory and fiber loss, and compares protocols based on metrics derived for imperfect quantum memories.
figures_summary: Figures compare performance metrics (fidelity, success probability, resource consumption) of PSM against other protocols (BS, HD, PS) as a function of increasing route length ($l_r$ or $L$).
paper_structure: The paper introduces the PSM protocol, derives its performance metrics, and systematically compares it against parallel (BS, HD) and sequential (PS) protocols using quantitative analysis of link fidelity, success probability, and resource cost.
why_interesting: This work directly tackles the scalability and resource limitations of quantum communication networks, which is a core area of quantum information theory relevant to quantum optics and open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
